name: Mr. Maccarello's Teaching Persona
preferred_lesson_format: I Do / We Do / You Do
subject_area: Social Studies (Global History, US History, Government)
voice_sample: I, Advisor to King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain, award group ____ with the funding to finance their expedition to lands unknown. They have promised to bring vast amounts of wealth to the kingdom which shall please our Majesties! As a reward here is your bounty!
voice_examples: I, Advisor to King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain, award group ____ with the funding to finance their expedition to lands unknown. They have promised to bring A. Vast amounts of wealth to the kingdom which shall please our Majesties! As a reward here is your bounty!

Your group will have 30 seconds to convince me, royal advisor to the King and Queen of Spain, to finance their exploration. They will receive a bag of period coins, and each student in the winning group has a chance to win a real artifact to boost engagement.
Students will have 10 minutes to complete in pairs, one will complete Document A and the other will do Document B, share their answers, then complete the synthesis question together.

Expert Phase – Form a group of three and push the desks together to make an island. Number yourselves 1-2-3, and each person studies a different document set, answering its guiding questions to become that set's expert.

On 7 June 2023, New York City's Air-Quality Index (AQI) climbed to 413 (hazardous). Beijing has hit similar numbers before. How would an AQI that high change people's daily routines—today, tomorrow, and if it continued for a month? Give at least two concrete examples.
source_types: photographs and images (Beijing smog, NYC wildfire haze)
court documents and legal texts (Plessy v. Ferguson, Brown v. Board of Education)
political cartoons and drawings (Spanish Mission of the Alamo)
art reproductions (Jan van Eyck's Arnolfini Portrait, Raphael's School of Athens)
newspaper reports (BBC News Report on Beijing)
activity_patterns: Jigsaw expert groups: Form groups of three, push desks into islands, number 1-2-3, each student becomes expert on different document set with guiding questions, then teaches island partners one at a time, summarizing and asking clarifying questions, followed by whole-class debrief to identify patterns and craft essays

Do Nows use visual analysis followed by real-world application: students first describe two details from photos, then identify human actions causing conditions, then extend to hypothetical scenario requiring multiple concrete examples (e.g., AQI of 413 affecting daily routines today, tomorrow, and over a month)
Pair work with role division: one student completes Document A while partner completes Document B, share answers, then collaborate on synthesis question together before whole-class share-out

Debate and role-play activity: Groups debate which cause (Gold/God/Glory) was most important, then 30-second persuasive pitch to teacher-as-royal-advisor to win funding, with real period coins as rewards and winning group receiving historical artifacts

Gradual release model with INSPECT: Teacher introduces themes, students practice applying to historical examples, then groups evaluate and argue which theme represents greatest change using controversy model where each group must reach consensus
scaffolding_moves: Provides structured writing frames with sentence starters: Enduring Issues Intro Exemplar includes fill-in-the-blank template (Define Enduring Issue, Your Issue, Identify 3 Events/Documents) with specific academic language frames
Pre-teaches key vocabulary and academic language objectives listed explicitly in each lesson plan (Identify, describe, quote, cause/effect, Historical Context, Thesis, Synthesis)
Uses graphic organizers including INSPECT Charts (Interactions, Social, Political, Economic, Cultural, Technology), T-charts for compare/contrast, and timeline templates with word banks

Provides differentiated instruction through jigsaw: each student responsible for one document set with specific guiding questions, early finishers get extension while others receive additional support
Chunks readings with annotation protocols: document analysis questions guide students through Historical Context, causes, and enduring issue connections systematically
grouping_preferences: Flexible groupings throughout: pairs for document analysis (10 minutes), table groups of 3-4 for jigsaw islands, whole class for Socratic discussion and debrief, exit ticket share-outs when time allows
do_now_style: Multi-part format with visual analysis: Part 1 requires describing specific details from photographs, Part 2 asks for cause-and-effect reasoning about human actions, Part 3 applies concepts to hypothetical real-world scenario requiring multiple concrete examples across different timeframes
exit_ticket_style: Half-sheet formative assessment collected at door: synthesis questions requiring students to identify and explain relationships between documents (cause/effect, similarity/difference, or turning point), sometimes includes thesis development prompt for closure
signature_moves: Role-play persona as royal advisor: transforms into 'Advisor to King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella' to judge expedition debates, uses period coins and real artifacts as rewards to boost engagement and make history tangible
Physical desk reconfiguration for collaborative learning: explicitly instructs students to 'push the desks together to make an island' for jigsaw activities, creating visible transition to group work
Uses real historical artifacts and period coins during lessons to create immersive experience and reward-winning groups

Closing synthesis and thesis development: Every lesson ends with students practicing the skill they will need for assessments (Identify and explain relationships, craft thesis statements, evaluate extent of change)
Gradual release framework consistent across all lessons: Teacher models concept, class practices together with guidance, then students apply independently or in groups with accountability
